refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure across Telemetry (sensor→operator) and Tasking (operator→vehicle) paths. Log packet loss statistics over extended operational sessions (≥8 hours) and validate against acceptance criterion.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands (Tasking) from tele-operator console to vehicle with <1% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle sensor telemetry (Telemetry) from vehicle to tele-operator console with <1% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert to Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall adapt or gracefully degrade operation when network conditions approach or exceed packet loss limits.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to maintain <1% aggregate packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive codec and compression with forward error correction (FEC) to recover lost packets and reduce effective packet loss impact.
DP-C: Implement network quality monitoring with dynamic command throttling and telemetry sampling rate reduction when packet loss exceeds threshold.
DP-D: Combine redundant channels (DP-A) with FEC (DP-B) and quality monitoring (DP-C) in an integrated communication resilience architecture.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement an integrated teleoperation communication resilience architecture combining: (1) dual independent network paths with automatic failover logic, (2) forward error correction (FEC) codec applied to Tasking and Telemetry streams, and (3) real-time network quality monitoring with adaptive payload reduction (command throttling, telemetry sampling) and SafetyAlert escalation when packet loss approaches customer threshold.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation control logic (command execution timing) with network adaptation logic (payload reduction). Risk: aggressive telemetry sampling reduction may degrade operator situational awareness, creating a secondary safety hazard. Mitigation: define minimum telemetry fidelity floor below which operation must be suspended.

DP-D couples Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface) with Teleoperation module. Risk: safety alert generation must be independent of network quality to avoid masking safety events. Mitigation: ensure SafetyAlert is generated locally on vehicle and tele-operator console, not dependent on bidirectional communication.

DP-D assumes availability of dual network infrastructure. Risk: customer site may not provide two independent network paths. Mitigation: clarify network assumptions in acceptance criteria negotiation.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite solution addressing packet loss via redundancy, error correction, and graceful degradation. Coupling is manageable if safety-critical alert generation is decoupled from network adaptation logic. Recommend explicit design rule: SafetyAlert generation shall not depend on successful transmission of Tasking or Telemetry.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer site provides or permits dual independent network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or two ISP connections). Risk: if only single network available, DP-D redundancy benefit is lost; recommend fallback to DP-B+DP-C.

Assumption: FEC overhead (typically 20–50% bandwidth increase) is acceptable within network capacity. Risk: if network is severely bandwidth-constrained, FEC may worsen congestion. Mitigation: conduct bandwidth budget analysis early.

Assumption: Adaptive payload reduction (telemetry sampling, command throttling) can be implemented without violating safety-critical control loop timing. Risk: if operator command latency becomes unpredictable, safety is compromised. Mitigation: define hard real-time bounds on command processing and enforce via scheduler.

Assumption: Network quality monitoring can reliably detect packet loss in real-time. Risk: packet loss may be bursty or asymmetric (uplink vs. downlink); detection lag may delay SafetyAlert. Mitigation: use multiple loss detection methods (ACK-based, probe-based) and set conservative thresholds.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Communication Resilience Layer (redundancy, FEC, monitoring) shall be transparent to application logic; (2) Safety Alert Generation shall be independent of network state; (3) Graceful Degradation (payload reduction) shall be triggered only after SafetyAlert and with explicit operator acknowledgment. This revision reduces coupling and clarifies safety boundaries.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication resilience architecture for remote equipment operation, including: (1) dual-path network management and failover logic, (2) FEC codec and compression for Tasking and Telemetry streams, (3) real-time network quality monitoring, (4) adaptive payload reduction (command throttling, telemetry sampling), and (5) SafetyAlert generation and escalation. The module interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking and Telemetry), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and Network Link (via dual physical/logical paths).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (steering, throttle, bucket control, etc.) transmitted from tele-operator console to Vehicle. Tasking stream is subject to FEC encoding and adaptive throttling. Expected packet loss target: <1%.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sensor data (position, orientation, load, engine state, camera feeds, etc.) transmitted from Vehicle to tele-operator console. Telemetry stream is subject to FEC encoding and adaptive sampling. Expected packet loss target: <1%.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts (e.g., 'Communication Quality Degraded', 'Packet Loss Exceeds Threshold', 'Failover Activated') transmitted to Safety Services and operator console. SafetyAlert generation is independent of network state to ensure safety events are never masked by communication failures.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Dual independent network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or dual ISP) providing physical/logical connectivity. Teleoperation module manages failover between paths and monitors quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) on each path.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse communication resilience logic across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), violating cohesion. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle owns the physical equipment and local sensors/actuators. While Vehicle generates Telemetry and consumes Tasking, the communication resilience logic (FEC, failover, quality monitoring) is not inherently a vehicle concern—it is a system-level communication concern. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would couple vehicle firmware with network adaptation, reducing modularity. Not recommended.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of end-to-end communication resilience. The module already owns the tele-operator console, command/telemetry processing, and network interface logic. DP-D (redundancy, FEC, quality monitoring, adaptive degradation) is cohesive with Teleoperation's mission. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all within or directly adjacent to Teleoperation scope. Highly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alert management. While Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert, it does not own the communication resilience logic that generates the alert. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would create a dependency inversion (safety module depends on communication module). Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D because: (1) it already owns the tele-operator console and command/telemetry processing logic, (2) communication resilience (redundancy, FEC, quality monitoring) is a core concern of remote operation, (3) all primary interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link) are within Teleoperation's scope, (4) SafetyAlert is generated by Teleoperation and consumed by Safety Services (a clean producer-consumer relationship), (5) assigning to Teleoperation avoids diffusion of communication logic across Vehicle and Safety Services modules.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it owns both application-level command/telemetry processing AND low-level network resilience logic. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (e.g., CommandProcessor, TelemetryProcessor, NetworkResilienceLayer) at the next decomposition level.

Risk: If Vehicle module is geographically distributed (e.g., multiple sensors/actuators on different hardware), Teleoperation may struggle to manage per-vehicle network state. Mitigation: define a clear Vehicle-side communication agent (e.g., Vehicle Communication Gateway) that Teleoperation interfaces with, abstracting vehicle-side complexity.

Risk: SafetyAlert generation in Teleoperation may create a circular dependency if Safety Services needs to control Teleoperation behavior (e.g., force shutdown). Mitigation: ensure SafetyAlert is unidirectional (Teleoperation → Safety Services) and Safety Services uses a separate control interface (not SafetyAlert) to command Teleoperation shutdown.

Risk: Dual network management (failover logic) may require real-time scheduling guarantees that conflict with other Teleoperation tasks. Mitigation: isolate failover logic in a dedicated thread/process with high priority and bounded latency.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assign DP-D to Teleoperation module with the following refinements: (1) Decompose Teleoperation into NetworkResilienceLayer (owns redundancy, FEC, failover, quality monitoring) and ApplicationLayer (owns command/telemetry processing). (2) Define a Vehicle Communication Gateway interface that abstracts vehicle-side network complexity. (3) Ensure SafetyAlert generation is independent of network state and uses a separate, high-priority alert channel. (4) Document network assumptions (dual-path availability, bandwidth budget, latency bounds) as acceptance criteria.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (integrated communication resilience architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤1%). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach (redundancy + FEC + quality monitoring + adaptive degradation) that, if implemented correctly, will achieve the acceptance criterion. The DP is neither over-constrained (it allows multiple implementation paths) nor under-constrained (it provides sufficient guidance). Coupling risks are identified and mitigatable. FR-DP mapping is sound.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If Teleoperation module is not properly isolated from Vehicle module, network resilience logic may leak into vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling. Mitigation: enforce clear module boundaries and use well-defined interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry).

Diffusion Risk 2: If SafetyAlert generation is not decoupled from network state, safety alerts may be lost during network failures, defeating the purpose of safety. Mitigation: implement SafetyAlert as a local, independent mechanism on both vehicle and console sides.

Diffusion Risk 3: If adaptive payload reduction (telemetry sampling, command throttling) is not carefully controlled, it may diffuse safety-critical decisions across multiple modules. Mitigation: centralize degradation policy in Teleoperation and enforce via explicit state machine.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment score (0.95) for DP-D. All primary interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are within or directly adjacent to Teleoperation scope. No significant diffusion expected if module boundaries are enforced. Recommend explicit design rule: Teleoperation shall not modify Vehicle firmware or Safety Services policy; it shall only generate SafetyAlert and manage network state.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation by simulating network conditions (packet loss, latency, jitter) and measuring Tasking/Telemetry delivery rates. Integration testing with Vehicle and Safety Services will validate SafetyAlert escalation and graceful degradation behavior. Acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss) is measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Teleoperation owns the end-to-end communication path and is responsible for achieving <1% packet loss. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) are allocated to specific Teleoperation sub-components (CommandProcessor, TelemetryProcessor, NetworkResilienceLayer, SafetyAlertGenerator) at the next decomposition level. Ownership and responsibility are unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly document network assumptions (dual-path availability, bandwidth budget, latency bounds, agreed network conditions) as part of acceptance criteria. Obtain customer sign-off on these assumptions before detailed design.

Revision 2: Define a minimum telemetry fidelity floor below which operation must be suspended (e.g., 'if telemetry sampling rate drops below 10 Hz, operator must manually disengage'). This prevents safety hazards from aggressive adaptive degradation.

Revision 3: Implement SafetyAlert as a separate, high-priority channel independent of Tasking/Telemetry streams. Ensure SafetyAlert can be transmitted even if Tasking/Telemetry channels are saturated.

Revision 4: Conduct a detailed FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) on the dual-path failover logic to identify single points of failure (e.g., failover decision logic itself). Ensure failover is deterministic and does not introduce new safety hazards.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) addressing command transmission, telemetry transmission, safety alert generation, and graceful degradation. DP-D is a composite solution that requires further decomposition into sub-DPs for each sub-FR. IM assignment to Teleoperation is sound and well-justified, but the module itself will require internal decomposition (NetworkResilienceLayer, ApplicationLayer, SafetyAlertGenerator) to manage complexity. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to detail the implementation architecture and allocate sub-FRs to sub-modules.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR-NGHE-009 explicitly lists four sub-FRs that are not yet allocated to specific design parameters or implementation modules. DP-D is a high-level architecture that must be decomposed into: (1) DP-D.1 (redundant network path management and failover), (2) DP-D.2 (FEC codec and compression), (3) DP-D.3 (network quality monitoring), and (4) DP-D.4 (adaptive payload reduction and SafetyAlert escalation). Each sub-DP will be assigned to a sub-module within Teleoperation. This decomposition is necessary to achieve sufficient detail for implementation and verification.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands (Tasking) from tele-operator console to vehicle with <1% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle sensor telemetry (Telemetry) from vehicle to tele-operator console with <1% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert to Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall adapt or gracefully degrade operation when network conditions approach or exceed packet loss limits.

assumptions: Customer site provides or permits dual independent network paths (e.g., cellular + satellite, or two ISP connections).
FEC overhead (20–50% bandwidth increase) is acceptable within network capacity.
Adaptive payload reduction can be implemented without violating safety-critical control loop timing.
Network quality monitoring can reliably detect packet loss in real-time with acceptable latency.
SafetyAlert generation can be decoupled from network state and implemented as an independent mechanism.
Teleoperation module can be decomposed into sub-modules (NetworkResilienceLayer, ApplicationLayer, SafetyAlertGenerator) without creating new coupling risks.
Vehicle module provides a clean communication interface (Tasking input, Telemetry output) that abstracts vehicle-side complexity.
open_risks: Risk: Dual network failover logic may introduce new single points of failure (e.g., failover decision logic itself). Mitigation: conduct detailed FMEA and implement redundant failover decision mechanisms.

Risk: Aggressive telemetry sampling reduction may degrade operator situational awareness, creating a secondary safety hazard. Mitigation: define minimum telemetry fidelity floor and enforce via explicit state machine.

Risk: SafetyAlert generation in Teleoperation may create circular dependencies with Safety Services. Mitigation: ensure SafetyAlert is unidirectional and Safety Services uses a separate control interface for shutdown commands.
Risk: Network quality monitoring may have detection lag, delaying SafetyAlert escalation. Mitigation: use multiple loss detection methods and set conservative thresholds.
Risk: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it owns both application-level and low-level network logic. Mitigation: decompose into sub-modules at next level.
evidence_needed: Customer sign-off on network assumptions (dual-path availability, bandwidth budget, latency bounds, agreed network conditions).
Bandwidth budget analysis confirming FEC overhead is acceptable.
Real-time scheduling analysis confirming adaptive payload reduction does not violate control loop timing.
Network quality monitoring design document with detection methods, thresholds, and latency bounds.
SafetyAlert design document with independence guarantees and escalation logic.
FMEA on dual-path failover logic identifying single points of failure and mitigation strategies.
Detailed decomposition of Teleoperation module into sub-modules (NetworkResilienceLayer, ApplicationLayer, SafetyAlertGenerator) with clear interfaces and ownership.
Integration test plan for Teleoperation with Vehicle and Safety Services, including network condition simulation and packet loss measurement.
